description: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait removed ~4.3M b/d from the market and triggered a price spike from ~$17 to over $40 within two months.
Collapse of the Thai baht sparked a regional crisis that crushed Asian oil demand, contributing to Brent falling below $10 by late 1998.
Attacks on the US caused an immediate price spike followed by a sharp decline as global economic activity and air travel contracted.
War in a major OPEC producer created supply uncertainty; prices rose in the run-up then stabilized after Iraqi supply disruption proved limited.
The GFC demand shock drove Brent from a July 2008 record of ~$147 to ~$34 by December 2008.
OPEC announced its largest-ever cut of 4.2M b/d (from Sept 2008 levels) to arrest the price collapse during the financial crisis.
Uprising in Libya removed ~1.5M b/d of light sweet crude, pushing Brent back above $100 and sustaining it there.
OPEC's decision to defend market share against US shale rather than cut output accelerated the 2014-2016 price collapse from ~$110 to under $30.
Lifting of nuclear-related sanctions returned ~1M b/d of Iranian exports to an already oversupplied market near the cycle low.
First OPEC production cut since 2008 (1.2M b/d, joined by non-OPEC producers) marked the start of the OPEC+ era and supported price recovery.

event_name: US withdraws from Iran nuclear deal
description: Re-imposition of US sanctions on Iranian exports tightened expected supply and supported prices through mid-2018.
Attack on Saudi Aramco facilities knocked out ~5.7M b/d (roughly 5% of world supply), causing the largest single-day Brent jump on record (~15%).
Russia's refusal to deepen cuts led Saudi Arabia to slash prices and raise output just as COVID-19 destroyed demand; Brent fell ~24% in a day.
Lockdowns collapsed global oil demand by ~20M b/d at the trough; Brent fell below $20 in April 2020, its lowest in two decades.
Largest coordinated production cut in history stabilized the market after the COVID crash and underpinned the 2020-2021 recovery.
Invasion by the world's second-largest crude exporter and ensuing sanctions pushed Brent above $130 in March 2022, the highest since 2008.
